meta.title: System Vision & Principles
meta.excerpt: Core architectural vision, design principles, and foundational values that guide all system decisions and technical direction.

seo.metaDescription: Learn the core architectural vision, design principles, and values that guide our Next.js documentation platform.
seo.keywords: architecture, system design, principles, vision, next.js, documentation

metaSocial.title: System Vision & Principles
metaSocial.description: Discover the architectural vision and design principles that guide our platform.

metaSocial.title: System Vision & Principles
metaSocial.description: Learn about our core architectural vision and design principles.

blocks.content: Our system is built on a clear architectural vision that prioritizes clarity, maintainability, and scalability. Every decision we make aligns with this vision and the principles derived from it.

We believe in building documentation platforms that are fast, accessible, and built on solid architectural foundations. Our 6-layer architecture (schema → builder → repository → viewmodels → facade → manifest) ensures that data flows cleanly through our application with no ambiguity about ownership or responsibility.

blocks.title: 6-Layer Architecture Model
blocks.content: This diagram (visual reference) shows how data flows from source through our layers, with each layer having a specific responsibility. See ARCHITECTURE.md for detailed explanation.
We follow three core principles that guide every architectural decision:

Data access, validation, and business logic live exclusively on the server. The Next.js App Router enforces this with 'use server' directives and import boundaries. This separation prevents client-side logic from leaking into data concerns and keeps our bundle small.

blocks.title: example-server-only.ts

blocks.content: Avoid: Never import 'server-only' modules in client components. TypeScript and import boundaries enforce this.
Every data layer is defined with TypeScript interfaces and validated with Zod schemas. This provides compile-time safety and runtime verification with no ambiguity.

blocks.title: schema-example.ts

blocks.content: We create new components only when existing ones cannot represent the requirement. Custom styling and prop variations are preferred over component proliferation.
Reuse mindset: Does a Button component with variant='primary' solve this, or do we really need a PrimaryButton component? Prefer the former.
Our technical values follow from these principles:

items: Data flows unidirectionally: extract → validate → transform → render
Every layer has explicit governance: schema files lock contract, checklist enforces gates
SEO is first-class: not deferred, not optional, integrated from day 1
Tests validate behavior, not implementation: focus on meaningful outcomes
Documentation is architecture: our docs follow the same 6-layer pattern we preach
Performance budgets are non-negotiable: we measure and enforce limits

blocks.content: These principles ensure that our system is maintainable, scalable, and accessible to new team members. They are not dogma—they can evolve, but only with explicit review and documentation.
